a window manager in a div
: a pretend operating system with draggable windows, a taskbar and a start menu. None of it is real. Every "window" is a
, and the whole window manager is about 150 lines of composable. Here's how it holds together.
There is no window object, only a row in an array:
s over the array and positions each window with an inline
. Rendering is Vue's problem; the composable only ever mutates numbers.
"Bring to front" is the oldest trick in the book: keep a monotonic counter and hand the focused window the next value.
. If focus is just "highest z", switching windows is sorting the open ones by
(Caveat: the OS usually eats real Alt+Tab before the page sees it. The taskbar stays the reliable switcher — this is a bonus for whoever's window manager doesn't grab it.)
records which window is being dragged and the grab offset. Then a
does the math for whichever window is currently active — no per-window listeners to add and tear down:
Aero-snap has a secret: the preview. Drag a window to the edge and Windows shows a translucent rectangle of where it will land
you let go. Without it, snapping feels like the app twitching.
based on how close the pointer is to a screen edge, and a computed turns that into a rectangle:
at that rect with a CSS transition on
, so it glides as you slide along the edge. On
decides whether to actually snap — the preview never lies, because it runs the exact function the drop does.
Maximizing and snapping both need an undo. Before either, we stash the current geometry; restoring pops it back:
Dragging a maximized window "peels" it off under the cursor — clear
, restore the old size, re-centre it on the pointer, keep dragging. Little touches like that are the difference between "divs that move" and something that feels like a desktop.

None of this is load-bearing. It's a portfolio easter egg. But building a window manager is one of those exercises where the real thing turns out to be a surprisingly small pile of good ideas — a counter, an array, and one honest preview.

dragging, snapping and alt-tab for lvOS — the fake desktop hiding in my portfolio — with one pointermove listener and a z-index counter.
giving my fake OS its own route
— the pretend desktop hiding in this site — started life as an
slams over the whole page at
. It worked. It also had two tells that it wasn't really a screen of its own.
The overlay covered the viewport, but the
page underneath it was still there

— hero, footer, all of it, taller than the screen. So the browser still showed a scrollbar. You could scroll a page you couldn't see, behind an OS that didn't move. A tiny thing, but it broke the illusion every time.
The honest fix isn't "hide the scrollbar." It's to stop having a tall page behind the desktop at all. That means the desktop shouldn't be an overlay on a page — it should
So lvOS moved to a real route,
, with a bare layout that renders nothing but the desktop:

<!-- pages/desktop.vue -->
<template>
  <ClientOnly>
    <WebDesktop />
  </ClientOnly>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
definePageMeta({ layout: 'desktop' })
useHead({ title: 'lvOS — Laurens Verspeek', htmlAttrs: { class: 'is-lvos' } })
</script>
No navbar, no footer, no background field — so there's no tall content to scroll. The
Scrollbar gone, because there's genuinely nothing to scroll.
. The old model needed a global
from the query string, a watcher to lazy-mount the component, and
sprinkled through every "log out" and "open this project" handler. All of it existed to fake navigation.
is a real route, navigation is navigation:
// booting the desktop is now just... going there
command, the command palette, and the footer link all
plugin got deleted. A whole piece of shared state evaporated, replaced by the router — which was always the right tool for "show a different screen."
And because it's a route, it's shareable:
boots you straight in. A taskbar button hands it to the real Fullscreen API for the full effect:
An overlay is a screen you're

is on top. Most of the time the pretense is free. But the moment the thing underneath can still scroll, or someone wants to link to it, the seams show. If a view is really its own place, give it its own route — the framework already knows how to get there.

lvOS was a fixed overlay with a dead scrollbar and no shareable URL. moving it to /desktop fixed both — and taught me where overlays quietly lie.
one Game of Life, four places
is Conway's Game of Life — a field of amber cells quietly evolving, that you can draw into and click to open
. It also runs as an ASCII toy in the
The trick to that isn't cleverness — it's
refusing to mix the rules with the pixels
the rules are 30 lines and no canvas
The simulation knows nothing about drawing, DOM, or Vue. It's a
— a blinker oscillates with period two, a block stays put, a lone cell dies. Those are unit tests, not something I eyeball in the browser:
The board wraps at the edges (
), which is why gliders sail off one side and reappear on the other instead of dying in a corner.
the canvas boilerplate is a composable

Every on-screen version needs the same tedious ceremony: size the canvas to its container, scale for the device pixel ratio, pause the loop when the tab is hidden or the visitor prefers reduced motion, and clean up on unmount. That's
/* size the grid, seed, draw one frame */

callbacks — the hero ticks on a fixed interval, the page paces itself off a speed slider — but neither re-implements resize or the animation loop. The ASCII terminal version skips the composable entirely; its "canvas" is a
When I wanted Life in a fourth place — a draggable lvOS window — it was an afternoon, not a project. Import the engine, hand
a draw function, add three buttons. No rules were rewritten, so no rules could drift out of sync between the hero and the window.
The general shape keeps showing up: the
part (Conway's four rules) is tiny and pure; the
part (canvas lifecycles) is boring and shared. Keep those two apart and the same idea renders anywhere you point it — even at a
the hero, a full page, an ASCII terminal toy and a desktop window all run the same 30-line Conway engine. a small lesson in keeping the rules pure.
putting a playable snake game in a fake terminal
. The fun constraint: the "screen" is just a
element in a Vue component. No canvas, no WebGL, just monospace text re-rendered 8 times per second.
the game is a string factory
A game here is a function that receives two callbacks: one to draw a frame, one to report the final output when the game ends.
Every tick, snake builds the whole board as a string — box-drawing characters for the walls,
for the food — and hands it to
, and because the font is monospace, the "pixels" line up perfectly:
Diffing a full-frame string sounds wasteful, but the frame is ~700 characters. The DOM update is nothing; your GPU yawns.
The terminal normally routes keys to an
. While a game is active, the input is hidden and a window-level listener feeds keys to the game instead:
for keys it consumed, so arrows stop scrolling the page but
end the game instead of closing the terminal — that one took a bug report from myself to get right.
When you die, the terminal prints:

game over — the snake has encountered a segfault
final score: 120
new high score! previous best: 80
, because if you're going to lose to a snake made of Unicode blocks, the least I can do is remember it.
. My high score is embarrassing, please beat it.

a game loop, a <pre> tag, and stealing the keyboard from vue — no canvas required.
rebuilding my portfolio after 4 years of "coming soon(ish)"
For four years, my personal website consisted of a hero, six project cards, and the sentence
"My personal slice of the web is still under construction and will be live if I can find some time to finish it ;)"
I never found the time. So instead of finishing v1, I deleted it.
. Bulma might seem like an odd choice next to the utility-css crowd, but v1 moved everything to CSS variables, which makes theming almost free:
element — Bulma swaps every variable, and anything custom reads
so it follows along. Even the canvas animations read their colors from the CSS variables at runtime.
The gimmick I actually wanted to build: press
and you get a terminal. It's not an emulator — it's a Vue component and a composable with a command map:

so the navbar button, the footer link and the overlay all talk to the same terminal. Commands can navigate the router, toggle the theme, fetch the GitHub API, or take over the keyboard entirely — which is how

), but the rule I set myself: every easter egg has to be discoverable from a hint somewhere else. The console hints at the terminal, the terminal hints at the hidden commands, the hidden commands hint at... you get it.
If you find one that isn't hinted at anywhere, that's a bug. Or is it?
